# Quantum Computing vs Fractal Compression In a Chaotic Discontinuum

By [Essam Abadir](mailto:essam@aspirevc.com)

**Abstract**

A formal proof is offered demonstrating that computational models relying on quantum superposition cannot outperform digital algorithms. The proof highlights the inherent limitations of continuous mathematics in a discontinuum and suggests that a paradigm shift toward discrete computational models such as fractal compression may be necessary to unlock new frontiers in both physics and computer science. Discussion of a potentially workable fractal compression framework is provided as exemplary of how certain apparent paradoxes are resolvable in a discontinuum and indicative that Einstein’s view of a dicontinuum may be fundamentally correct. 

**Introduction**

| “However, you have also understood correctly the disadvantage attached to a continuum. … The question seems to me to be how one can formulate statements about a discontinuum without resorting to a continuum (space-time); the latter would have to be banished from the theory as an extra construction that is not justified by the essence of the problem and that corresponds to nothing “real.” But for this we unfortunately are still lacking the mathematical form. How much I have toiled in this direction already\!\!” *\-Einstein,* [1917 letter to Hans Walter Dällenbach](https://einsteinpapers.press.princeton.edu/vol8-trans/314) (emphasis added). |
| :---- |

Quantum computing, predicated on a continuum of space-time, could be an advance that is potentially more meaningful than any other in history. However, we now advance the argument that the framework of space-time continuum on which it is based is fundamentally flawed and that there is likely a better path to the same technological destination to which quantum computing would take us — a path that is presently attainable with digital computers and which is free of the many baffling paradoxes spawned by the continuum assumption. Rooted in discrete mathematics that digital computers excel at, it is shown that fractal compression is, at minimum, a mathematical form that is equally capable to calculus and, in the case of a discontinuum, potentially far more powerful. The inspiration for this fractal compression path is the simple casting of Einstein’s own theories from his 1905 papers under the assumption of a discontinuum.

Since the turn of the 20th century, mainstream physics has assumed that space and time are continuous despite reservations such as those expressed by Einstein in his letter to Dallenbach. As Schrödinger also tried to show with absurdity of a cat that was both dead and alive, something fundamental was amiss. It is no secret that our universe is fundamentally chaotic, and as proven by Poincare, the father of Chaos Theory, no calculus-based closed-form system of equations can describe chaotic systems. Similarly, if the universe is made up of discrete bodies and those bodies are subject to attractive fundamental forces, then every physics problem is a form of the N-body problem, and calculus cannot be a mathematical language that can correctly describe the physics of our universe. If one were to attempt to use only calculus, such as ordinary differential equations or partial differential equations, to describe all of physics, then the theory must be either incomplete or inconsistent, and absurdities would occur. As Schrödinger attempted to point out, wave particle duality and quantum superposition are just the types of absurdities that one might expect.

Shortly after the turn of the 20th century, Einstein, like Schrödinger, was deeply troubled by the unmoored construction of reality that the continuous mathematics of calculus requires. Much to Einstein’s frustration, he was unable to find alternative tools to calculus, but now, with the great advancement of digital computing, the mathematical forms that Einstein hoped for are achievable.

We now re-explore physics calculations from a perspective that we call fractal compression for discrete methods drawn from chaos theory, information theory, stochastic processes, computational theory, and discrete complexity. Relying heavily on Einstein’s insights in his famous 1905 papers, we recast them within the context of a discontinuum and layout the case for fractal compression rather than quantum computing, which is the mathematical form of physics. Formal proofs are offered in the addendum.

**Discussion**

In the addendum, we give formal logic proofs of the statements relating to “... how one can formulate statements about a discontinuum without resorting to a continuum (space-time)” and show that in the case of a discontinuum, we have the necessary mathematical form Einstein sought over a century ago. The proofs also show that in the case of a discontinuum superposition based qubits as the premise of quantum computing would be fatally flawed. The first part of this discussion gives the overall argument which is formalized in the proofs of the addendum.

The remainder of the discussion focuses on the latter part of Einstein’s statement regarding why a contiunuum “ … would have to be banished from the theory as an extra construction that is not justified by the essence of the problem and that corresponds to nothing ‘real.’” For these purposes, paradoxes like wave-particle duality and quantum entanglement are explored and how the perception of the “unreal” under a continuum assumption might arise from real configurations in an actual discontinuum. Accordingly, the reader should neither equate the latter part of the discussion to the claim that space-time is *proven* to be a discontinuum nor that the discrete algorithms discussed below are a complete physics model that replicates all current physics theories.

By proceeding around the more narrow discussion around quantum computing, we will explore the possibility of Einstein’s mathematical form. Quantum supremacy is the notion that a quantum computer can perform a calculation that cannot be performed on a digital computer in the same amount of time. In exploring the possibility of quantum supremacy, we should first see if it is built on a faulty foundation. We begin with the idea that the mathematical framework for computing physics should be contradiction free; that is, it should not lead to absurdities. One hopes that this approach might even be intuitive. Accordingly, the formal logic proofs that are offered in the addendum are discussed in more accessible terms herein. In the context of a fundamental discontinuum assumption, the overall argument is rather simple:

1. **Universal N-Body Problem:** *In a discrete universe* where we observe attractive forces and bodies can be in only one place at a time, then every physics problem is a form of the popularly known “3 Body Problem” (or N-Body Problem), which is not solvable with calculus;  
2. **Digital Supremacy:** If the universe is discrete and calculus cannot describe it, then there is at least one form of discrete mathematical algorithm, fractal compression, that can describe everything calculus can describe at least as well calculus does;  
3. **Quantum Superposition Invalid:** If the universe is discrete, then fractal compression, which is computationally equivalent to quantum’s calculus equations, does not allow one body to be in more than one place at one time and would therefore be able to explain away the contradiction of quantum superposition that a body is in more than one place at one time.  
4. **Conclusion, No Quantum Computer Possible:** If quantum superposition cannot exist in a discrete universe, then a quantum supercomputer cannot be built on that principle; however, *if* there are any computing gains to be made, then fractal compression will be able to achieve them.

Therefore, as formally shown in the addendum, quantum computing based on superposition is not possible in discrete space-time where attractive forces exist at every scale of reality since, by definition, every thing in reality is a discrete body and every physics problem is a form of the N-body problem.

Since the logic proofs of the addendum are proof only of the existence of fractal compression in a discontinuum, they do not propose an actual fractal compression algorithm. Since it would be satisfying to see how even an incomplete fractal compression algorithm might work in parts to provide intuitive resolution of otherwise inexplicable phenomena that occur in an assumed continuum, the remainder of the discussion focuses on these areas.

### Fractal Algorithms and Scale Invariant Physics—The Einstein Newton Laws

By "scale invariant" physics, we mean that the rules governing the motion of bodies are invariant to the scale of the bodies involved. For example, if the bodies are planets, atoms, or quarks, then the rules governing what they will do will be the same. This is in contrast to the current dichotomy between scales where the fundamental forces are thought to be distinct and scale dependent. Likewise, scale invariance lies in contrast to wave‒particle duality at the quantum scale and discrete classical dynamics at larger scales.

Compared to theories, laws give no motivation for their being so. Theories, on the other hand, give us intuition as to the complex ways these laws will express themselves in our physics-based reality. Under a scale invariant set of laws that would bridge the motion of the very largest bodies, such as planets, with those of the very smallest bodies, such as electrons, it is proposed that we recast certain of Einstein’s theories as laws. Specifically, from Einstein's publication during his Annus Mirabilis in 1905, we will reformulate and elaborate Brownian motion, relativity frames, and quantization of energy as laws that stand as additions to Newton's Laws of Motion (collectively the "Einstein Newton Laws").

Therefore, for our scale invariant laws, we will convert Einstein’s 1905 theories to laws and take them as givens along with Einstein’s then implicit belief in a discrete particle nature of the universe. One way to achieve scale invariance is to describe a recursive or “fractal” relationship such that the patterns that appear when we zoom into the smallest scales are the same patterns we see when we zoom out to the largest scales. Defining such a fractal relationship puts us on a path to fractal compression.

### Definition of Fractal Compression

Fractal compression is defined as a discrete algorithm operating in a discontinuum and satisfying the following conditions:

* (S2P1) The algorithm operates on a discrete grid where a subgrid of points is considered a box (“frame”), each body is considered a ball (a child of a frame), and each frame can be considered a body (“ball”), which is the child of a larger frame.  
  * (S2P2) The bodies (balls) in the universe are guaranteed to take on every “balls into boxes” configuration at some point.  
  * (S2P3) Within any frame, the laws of the universe converge to oscillatory motion of the body children of the frame in polynomial time.  
  * (S2P4) For every sine or cosine function, there is a subset (or “frame”) of the universe’s grid where the oscillatory body movements describe that function.  
  * (S2P5) The frames can be superimposed in an additive manner.  
  * (S2P6) The rules of physics are scale invariant such that the dynamics and forces that apply at large scales governing celestial motion also apply at the quantum scale.

### The Einstein–Newton Laws As Fractal Algorithm:

Since our aim is only to show how discrete algorithms with certain properties can intuitively explain phenomena such as wave‒particle duality, we make no claim as to whether the Einstein–Newton Laws below satisfy all the conditions for fractal compression. Hence, we will give them the lesser label of “fractal algorithm” and discuss several perceived paradoxes on which they will shed light.

1\. **Discrete Space and Time (S2P1):** The universe is a discrete grid with a minimum unit of distance (a "pixel") and a minimum unit of time (a "tick"), which is the time it takes for a body to move one pixel. No two bodies can occupy the same pixel at the same time.

2\. **Relativity Frame (S2P1 & S2P5):** Each body has a parent body that is a "relativity frame" for the body. If the parent frame of the body moves by some number of pixels, then the child body moves the same relative amount. Relativity frames are hierarchical, meaning that a child in a frame is a body relative to its parent and that the same child is a frame relative to its children. The largest relativity frame is the universe itself.

3\. **Brownian Motion (S2P2 & S2P6):** Within the parent frame, for each tick, each body randomly moves one pixel in every direction available to it (e.g., in a 2D space, \+/- one pixel in x and y).

4\. **Quantization of Energy (S2P1)**: The energy of a body is quantized in units measured by the number of pixels occupied in the body.

5\. **Attractive Forces in Relativity Frame (S2P1):** Each body moves one pixel per tick toward the center of its relativity frame.

6\. **Bodies In Motion Tend to Stay in Motion:** Each body will continue to move in the same direction unless acted upon by the attractive force of its parent frame or by a collision with another child of the same parent frame.

7\. **Conservation of Energy:** The total energy of the universe is conserved.

### Oscillatory Motion, Orbital Motion, & Entropy

Frame 1 is assumed, as shown in Fig. 1, with two indistinguishable bodies A and B. Fig. 1 walks through a hypothetical progression of the movement of the bodies in the frame while observing the Einstein–Newton Laws. The purple arrows are indicative of the attractive force (law 5\) within the frame (not indicative of magnitude), and the red arrows on the bodies are indicative of Brownian motion (law 3), which randomly sends the balls in one pixel in both the x and y directions. When inertia is taken into account (law 6\) and because the two bodies cannot occupy the same square (law 1), the dynamics of the system indicate that the bodies have a tendency to both spread out (entropy) and to oscillate in two dimensions around the center (orbital motion). While we give no formal proof, one can conclude that the frequency of oscillation or rotaion is related to the size of the frame and the number of bodies it contains, and for appropriate choices of each, any oscillation frequency can be achieved. From a scale invariant perspective, the same dynamics which would display as celestial motion at very large scales, would emerge as electron orbits at the atomic scale, and wave particle duality at the quantum scale or even smaller.

| ![][image1] |
| :---- |
| **Fig. 1:** Frame 1 (in gold outline), with two bodies Body A and Body B around Frame 1’s center at Tick 0 and a hypothetical progression for several ticks. |

### Wave Particle Duality

In Fig. 1, one can also see that bodies starting on dark squares will stay on dark squares as a result of the body moving \+/- 1 pixel in both the x and y directions.

Essentially, we can think of the frame configuration of bodies as representing a sort of weighted coin whose probability rotates according to the body positions within the frame. The time evolution of the position of a body whose movement is determined by such a coin flip is called a random walk in the field of stochastic processes.

| ![][image2] | ![][image3] |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Fig. 2:** Scale Invariance and frame hierarchy example \- Frame 1 as a body within some larger parent Frame 2\. As we zoom out, the bodies in Frame 1 become “invisible.” | **Fig. 2A:** Scale Invariance and frame hierarchy example \- a potential zoomed in view of Body A in Fig 1\. |

For illustrative purposes, we call this weighted coin flip random walk the “Particle Walk” and animate according to the following principles under the assumption that a particle is like a body within a larger frame, as shown in Fig. 2\.

***Particle Walk:***

1. **Electronic Graph Paper Checkerboard, Universal Clock Tick, and Particle Pieces:** The smallest piece in the game is called a ***particle***. At each turn of the game, one ***tick*** on the universal clock occurs. Particles move one square every tick of the universe’s clock unless they receive extra moves, as described in other rules.  
2. **White Squares/Lighter Particles, Dark Squares/Darker Particles:** A particle which starts on a white square always stays on white squares—e.g., a piece trying to move right to left along the X-axis will zig-zag around the X-axis, which is its centerline of movement.  
3. **Primary Direction, Weighted Coin Random Zigzag,** A particle has a ***primary direction*** toward the center of its parent frame; in this case, we have said that many squares are away. Since particles cannot move to the wrong color squares, the particle must zigzag around its primary direction by picking its next closest same color square at random. For example, if a single particle is moving left to right along the X axis, it will pick its next greater x zig-zag square at random as if it were flipping a ***weighted coin*** (greater heads than tails); if that particle is starting from a dark square, its first set of moves will likely be up; otherwise, if it started from a white square, its first set of moves is likely to be downward. The weight of the coin will flip-flop periodically based on the oscillation frequency of the body of the parent frame. That is, the coin will have a greater probability of heads for n moves and then a greater probability of tails for n moves; we call 2n the “***wavelength***”.  
4. **White Spin-Down, Dark Spin-Up:** Two particles moving in the same direction, starting right next to each other, must be of different colors, and when this happens, they tend to move away from each other. We will therefore, somewhat arbitrarily, say that white particles are spin-down and that dark particles are spin-up.

For example, if we were to trace the route of a single black piece over twenty ticks and if the piece was moving from left to right, the path might take:

|  ![][image4] |
| :---- |
| ***Fig-3: Particle Walk Example of a spin-up particle starting on a dark square over 20 ticks simulated on a Google Sheet*** The particle has wavelength 8, so every 4 moves its probailty of moving up or down flips relative to its primary direction. |

The path of this one particle over time seems to already be making some sort of wave-like pattern similar to how physicists say light waves move in *Picture-1* below.

|  ![][image5] |
| :---- |
| ***Picture-1:*** Ray of light, moving “straight” moves up and down as a sinusoid. |

If we animate thousands of such particles as pixels on a computer screen where individual pixels are hard to see and when we zoom far away, the initial checkerboard essentially becomes invisible, but the stream of particles after a few seconds (where a second is 60 ticks) looks like *Picture-2* (wavelength \= 64 for visual clarity):

| ![][image6] |
| :---- |
| ***Picture-2:*** Simulation of thousands of particles doing a probablistic Particle walk |

### Superposition

In Picture-2, it is very difficult to identify individual pixel particles, and it would be completely impossible to do so if there were quadrillions of particles moving at a light speed. The particles would be so small that they would be completely invisible on an individual basis. While we could characterize the stream of particles as a wave, we could not “collapse the wavefunction” without colliding the invisible particles into some sort of detector. When we did so, we would have complete information about some of the particles’ positions (because we detected them), but we would have lost information about their momentum because we would not know what each one’s likely next move would be. On the other hand, if we started a stream of particles from a known location, the longer we waited to detect them by colliding them into our detector, the better our information about their momentum was; however, because of Brownian motion, we would have worse information about the exact locations of individual particles. In quantum mechanics, this is the Heisenberg uncertainty principle, which states that the particles are in all possible positions with a certain probability, i.e., they are in a state of superposition. Of course, in a discontinuum, we readily see that the particles are in a specific position and that the perceived mystery of superposition does not exist.

### Entanglement

To explain entanglement, let us start two particles moving in the \+X direction at the same time on adjacent white and black squares. These particles tend to move with each other as if they were a mirror image through the X-axis.

|  ![][image7] |
| :---- |
| ***Fig-5: Entanglement Pattern \-example path of a spin-up particle (the red) and a spin-down particle (the yellow) starting adjacent dark and light squares square over 20 ticks, simulated on a Google Sheet*** |

Fig-5 might not seem like a great revelation at this point, but in fact, it is the full explanation of what physicists call entanglement. Since the nonphysicist reader is likely to be less confused than the physicist at this point, it might be useful to play a little game. In this game, your job is to guess the position of both particles on move N, but you are only allowed to ask one of the following questions:

- On move N-1, what is the position of one of the particles at a particular time? Or,  
- From move N-2 to N-1 in a certain region, was there a particle that moved up or down?

For example, if on move 17 in Fig 3 you needed to guess the positions of particles you might ask to be given the precise location information of one particle and be told that on move 16 there was a particle on square \-1,16. You would likely deduce that there was likely another particle close to \-1,16 but you wouldn’t have any information on which was red or gold. This does not mean that the gold particle caused the red particle to approach it just because the gold particle was measured.

Similarly, if you asked about the movement of one of the particles from move 15 to move 16 and were told “a particle below the X-axis moved up, you would likely guess that the gold particle was the particle below the X-Axis but you would not have an exact idea of where the red particle was positioned.

### Diffusion & Fractal Interference

In Particle Walk, we observed that while the *average* movement of the particles was a sinusoidal wave, each individual particle was randomly distributed around the average in *Picture 2*. If we zoom-in really to the particle wave, the smooth line into clumpy groups of particles and the farther we zoom-out from the light source, the more diffuse the particles are from the average sinusoid.

| ![][image8] |
| :---- |
| ***Picture-8:*** Zoomed view of Particle Walk as in Fig-4 |

### Scales Smaller Than Photons, Frames, & Emergent Color

When we draw frames around these clumps, the frames will have different fills, and to easily keep track of how to fill each frame, the frames might be colored as in Picture-9, where the red bordered frame is the least full, the pink frame is the most full, and the blue frame is less full than the pink frame.

| ![][image9] |
| :---- |
| ***Picture-9:*** Candidate Frames with different fills denoted by the different color borders. |

As we saw before, frames that are fuller have shorter wavelengths, but frames that are less full have longer wavelengths.

| ![][image10] | ![][image11] |
| :---- | :---- |
| ***Picture-10:*** Zoomed Out Colorized Light Frames of Different Wavelengths | ***Picture-11:*** Zoomed In Colorized Light Frames of Different Wavelengths |

*Pictures-10* and *Picture-11* are screen captures from a simulation running at 72 fps in a web browser simulating tens of thousands of particles. Orange and red wavelengths have longer wavelengths, as expected, as shown in *Picture-10*, while in *Picture-11,* it is slightly easier to see the Particle Walk movement pattern of the pink frames.

## **Conclusion:**

In the addendum, we have given formal logic proofs of the statements relating to “... how one can formulate statements about a discontinuum without resorting to a continuum (space-time).” While we would like to go as far as Einstein and say that “... the latter would have to be banished from the theory as an extra construction that is not justified by the essence of the problem and that corresponds to nothing ‘real.’”, we will constrain ourselves to say that the contradictions born of a continuum cast grave doubt as to whether quantum computing is something real. We will leave it to the reader to decide whether the intuitive results of the presented fractal algorithm point toward fractal compression as a better path.

## **Methods & Addendum:**

Given the assumption of a discontinuum, the following statements are proven resulting in conclusion that a fractal compression will meet or exceed any quantum computing approach premised on qubits and quantum superposition:

* **S1 – No Continuous Mathematical Model For A Discontinuum:** ∄ CPCL, where CPCL is a PCL using continuous mathematics to model F that satisfies the convergence criterion for all N \> N0, where N0 is a sufficiently large number.

* **S2 – Fractal Compression At Least As Good As Any Continuous Model:** For any continuous physics theory T whose fundamental equations are expressible as a finite set of integral, bounded, and periodic functions, a fractal compression (satisfying S2P1-S2P6) can, at minimum, approximate the solutions to those equations within an arbitrary error tolerance ε \> 0\.

* **S3 – Superposition Contradiction In Continuous Model, Fractal Compression Is Contradiction Free:** Let D \= (P, T, B, F, C) be a discontinuum (as defined previously).

  * **(a) Quantum Mechanics Contradiction:** A direct interpretation of a continuous wave function Ψ(x, t) within D, where |Ψ(x, t)|2 represents the probability density of finding a particle at position x at time t, leads to a logical contradiction.

  * **(b) Fractal Compression Consistency:** A fractal compression algorithm A, satisfying properties S2P1-S2P6, can simulate the observable phenomena attributed to a particle appearing to occupy multiple locations at a time within D without leading to the contradiction described in part (a).

* **S4 – Fractal Compression Can Not Be Outperformed By Quantum Computer Based On Superposition:** Let D be a discontinuum.

  * **(a) Superposition Limitation:** No computational model based solely on the principle of quantum superposition can outperform a classical computational model in D.

  * **(b) Fractal Compression Potential:** If any computational advantages exist in D beyond those achievable by a classical computational model, a fractal compression algorithm satisfying S2P1-S2P6 can achieve them.

**I. Definitions**

* **Discontinuum:** A model of spacetime D \= (P, T, B, F, C), where  
  * **P:** A finite set of discrete points representing space, called "pixels."  
  * **T:** A discrete set representing time, where each element represents a "tick," and ti+1 \- ti \= Δt (a constant) for all ti, ti+1 ∈ T.  
  * **B:** A finite set of "bodies," where each b ∈ B occupies a subset of pixels Pb ⊆ P at any given time t ∈ T.  
  * **F:** A set of functions representing physical laws, acting on bodies B over time T within the space P. This set includes an attractive force function Fa: C x T → ℝ3.  
  * **C ⊆ ℘(B):** A set of subsets of B, where each c ∈ C represents a collection of bodies that can experience the attractive force Fa collectively. We call these each such c a "frame."  
* **Physics Computing Language (PCL):** An algorithm A(D, ε, N) with input D (discontinuum), ε (error tolerance), and N (number of bodies) satisfying:  
  * **Convergence:** ∀ε \> 0, ∃δ \> 0 such that for all Δt \< δ, the difference between A(D, ε, N) and the expected solution at any time t is less than ε.  
  * **Computable:** A(D, ε, N) halts and produces an output for any valid input (D, ε, N) in a finite number of steps.  
  * **Stable:** Small perturbations in the initial conditions of D result in bounded changes in the output of A(D, ε, N) over time.  
  * **Efficient:** The temporal and spatial complexity of A(D, ε, N) scales polynomially with N.  
* **Fractal Compression:** A type of algorithm that operates in D by incorporating the properties S2P1 to S2P6 (formalized as needed later).

**II. Axioms** 

* **A1 (Discrete Universe):** The universe can be modeled as a discontinuum D \= (P, T, B, F, C).  
* **A2 (Attractive Force and Frames):**  
  * **Existence:** ∃ c ∈ C where |c| ≥ 3 (at least one frame exists containing three or more bodies).  
  * **Non-zero Force:** For any c ∈ C and |c| ≥ 3, there exists a time interval where ||Fa(c, t)|| \> 0\.

**III. Proofs**

1. **Statement 1 (S1)**

* **S1:** ∄ CPCL, where CPCL is a PCL using continuous mathematics to model F that satisfies the convergence criterion for all N \> N0, where N0 is a sufficiently large number.

**Corollaries to S1**

* **Chaotic Nature (CS1A):** Given A1 and A2, the evolution of system D over time constitutes a chaotic system as defined by chaos theory, exhibiting sensitive dependence on initial conditions. This arises due to the nonlinear interactions governed by Fa.  
* **Convergence requirement (CS1B):** For any PCL candidate A' to be considered a valid model of physics within a discontinuum D, A' must satisfy the convergence criterion for the N-body problem as N becomes large, as dictated by the chaotic nature of the system.


**Proof of S1**

1. **Assumption:** Suppose, for the sake of contradiction, that S1 is false.  
   * This means: ∃ CPCL (a continuous-mathematics-based PCL) that satisfies the convergence criterion for all N \> N0, where N is the number of bodies in the system.  
2. **CPCL and Frames:** To model the attractive force Fa acting on sprites/frames (as defined in our discontinuum D), CPCL must represent the collective behavior of the bodies within those frames using continuous functions over continuous time.  
3. **Non-Integrable N-Body Problem:** By Axiom A2, at least one frame (c ∈ C) exists containing three or more bodies (|c| ≥ 3), and this frame experiences a nonzero attractive force. The behavior of bodies within this frame is governed by the dynamics of the N-body problem, where N \= |c|. Even if CPCL models individual bodies as point particles, the attractive force Fa acting on the frame as a whole introduces the complexity of the N-body problem within that frame.  
   * For  N ≥ 3 , Henri Poincaré proved that is non-integrable, meaning no general solution exists in terms of elementary functions or finite combinations thereof[^1].

   **Implications:**

* **No Closed-Form Solutions:** Continuous mathematics cannot provide exact analytical solutions for the NNN-body problem when N≥3N.  
* **Complex Dynamics:** The system exhibits sensitive dependence on initial conditions and complex trajectories.  
4. **Analytical Methods Insufficient:**  
   * Since no general analytical solutions exist, the CPCL cannot rely on closed-form expressions to model the system accurately. Utilizing numerical methods in the CPCL would mean the CPCL is not using continuous mathematics exclusively, contradicting its definition.  
   * Numerical & Continuous Approximation Methods Contradict CPCL:  
     1. Numerical methods approximate solutions by discretizing time and/or space, leading to stepwise computations.  
     2. Methods like perturbation theory or series expansions may be employed but are limited in scope and convergence.  
     3. These methods often rely on assumptions (e.g., small perturbations) that do not hold for arbitrary NNN and arbitrary initial conditions.  
5. **Contradiction:** Our assumption that such a CPCL exists leads to a contradiction because continuous mathematics cannot provide the necessary solutions.   
   * **Conclusion:** No CPCL exists that models F in D and is convergent for all N \> N0.  
   * **Therefore:** Our initial assumption must be false. This proves S1: In a discontinuum, no PCL based solely on continuous mathematics can guarantee convergence for modeling the attractive force Fa as the number of bodies within frames/sprites increases.

2. **Statement S2**

**S2:** For any continuous physics theory T whose fundamental equations are expressible as a finite set of integral, bounded, and periodic functions, a fractal compression (satisfying S2P1-S2P6) can, at minimum, approximate the solutions to those equations within an arbitrary error tolerance ε \> 0\.

* (S2P1) The algorithm operates on a discrete grid where a subgrid of points is considered a box (“frame”), each body is considered a ball (a child of a frame), and each frame can be considered a body (“ball”), which is the child of a larger frame.  
  * (S2P2) The bodies (balls) in the universe are guaranteed to take on every “balls into boxes” configuration at some point.  
  * (S2P3) Within any frame, the laws of the universe converge to oscillatory motion of the body children of the frame in polynomial time.  
  * (S2P4) For every sine or cosine function, there is a subset (or “frame”) of the universe’s grid where the oscillatory body movements describe that function.  
  * (S2P5) The frames can be superimposed in an additive manner.  
  * (S2P6) The rules of physics are scale invariant such that the dynamics and forces that apply at large scales governing celestial motion also apply at the quantum scale.

**Proof of S2**

1. **Assume a Continuous Physics Theory:** Let T be a continuous physics theory whose fundamental equations are expressible as a finite set { f1 (x, t), f2 (x, t), ..., fn (x, t)} of integral, bounded, and periodic functions. These functions represent the evolution of physical quantities (e.g., position, momentum, fields) over space (x) and time (t).  
2. **Fractal Compression Properties:** Assume that we have a fractal compression algorithm A satisfying properties S2P1-S2P6.  
3. **Fourier Series Representation:** By the fundamental theorem of Fourier series, each function fi (x, t) in the set can be represented as a convergent Fourier series:  
   fi (x, t) \= a0 \+ Σ(ak \* cos(kxωt) \+ bk \* sin(kxωt))  
   where ak and bk are the Fourier coefficients, ω is the fundamental frequency, and the summation is over all integer values of k.  
4. **Approximating Individual Terms (S2P4 & S2P5):** By S2P4, the oscillatory motion of bodies within frames in our fractal compression can represent any sine or cosine function. By S2P5, these frames can be superimposed additively. Therefore, for any individual term in the Fourier series of fi (x, t), we can configure frames and bodies in D such that their motion approximates that term within some error bound.  
5. **Approximating the Entire Function:** Since the Fourier series of fi (x, t) converges, we can approximate the function to an arbitrary accuracy εi \> 0 by taking a finite number of terms in the series. By combining the approximations of these individual terms (using superposition of frames as in S2P5), the fractal compression A can approximate the entire function fi (x, t) within εi.  
6. **Approximating the System of Equations:** This process can be repeated for each function fi (x, t) in the finite set defining the physics theory T. Fractal compression can therefore approximate the solutions to the entire system of equations simultaneously, with each function approximated within its corresponding εi. By choosing appropriate configurations of frames and bodies, the overall error in approximating the solutions to T can be made arbitrarily small (less than ε).  
7. **Therefore,** a fractal compression satisfying S2P1-S2P6 can, at minimum, approximate the solutions to any continuous physics theory whose fundamental equations are expressible as integral, bounded, and periodic functions within an arbitrary error tolerance.

3. **Statement S3**

**S3:** Let D \= (P, T, B, F, C) be a discontinuum (as defined previously).

* **(a) Quantum Mechanics Contradiction:** A direct interpretation of a continuous wave function Ψ(x, t) within D, where |Ψ(x, t)|2 represents the probability density of finding a particle at position x at time t, leads to a logical contradiction.  
* **(b) Fractal Compression Consistency:** A fractal compression algorithm A, satisfying properties S2P1-S2P6, can simulate the observable phenomena attributed to a particle appearing to occupy multiple locations at a time within D without leading to the contradiction described in part (a).

**Proof of S3**

**Part (a):**

1. **Continuous Wave Function:** A continuous wave function Ψ(x, t) implies that for any given time t, the probability density |Ψ(x, t)|2 is defined for all points x in space.  
2. **Discontinuum Restriction:** In a discontinuum D, a body b ∈ B can only occupy a subset of discrete pixels Pb ⊆ P at any given time t.  
3. **Contradiction:** If we directly interpret |Ψ(x, t)|2 as the probability of finding a body at a point x, then for a time t, there could be multiple pixels x ∈ P where |Ψ(x, t)|2 \> 0\. This contradicts the restriction imposed by the discontinuum that a body can occupy only one set of pixels at a time.

**Part (b):**

1. **Fractal Compression Simulation:** While the specific details of a fractal compression algorithm A are not defined in this statement, we know from S2 that A can approximate the solutions of relevant physics theories (which might include quantum phenomena) within an arbitrary error tolerance.  
2. **Discrete Representation:** By its definition, a fractal compression represents physical quantities discretely using frames and bodies within the discontinuum D.  
3. **Emergent Behavior:** The observable phenomena attributed to a particle appearing in multiple locations (e.g., interference patterns) can be interpreted as emergent consequences of the underlying discrete dynamics of frames and bodies within A, governed by the properties S2P1-S2P6.  
4. **Absence of Contradiction:** Since A operates fundamentally within the discrete framework of D, it inherently avoids the contradiction described in part (a). A body within A can only occupy a single state (represented by its position within its frame) at any given time, even if the emergent behavior at a larger scale gives the illusion of multiple simultaneous positions.

4. **Statement S4**

**S4:** Let D be a discontinuum.

* **(a) Superposition Limitation:** No computational model based solely on the principle of quantum superposition can outperform a classical computational model in D.  
* **(b) Fractal Compression Potential:** If any computational advantages exist in D beyond those achievable by a classical computational model, a fractal compression algorithm satisfying S2P1-S2P6 can achieve them.

**Proof of S4**

**Part (a) \- Superposition Limitation**

1. **Assumption (for Contradiction):** Suppose, for the sake of contradiction, that there exists a computational model M in D such that  
   * **(M1)** Operates solely based on the principle of quantum superposition (i.e., its fundamental unit of information is a qubit that can exist in a superposition of states, and its computations are based on the evolution and measurement of these superpositions).  
   * **(M2)** Outperforms all classical computational models for some problem P in terms of time complexity (i.e., M can solve P asymptotically faster than any classical algorithm).  
2. **Contradiction from S3(a):** By statement S3(a), a direct interpretation of a continuous wave function (which is essential for describing quantum superpositions) within a discontinuum D leads to a logical contradiction. As M relies solely on quantum superposition (M1) and operates within D, M would also inherently embody this logical contradiction.  
3. **Conclusion (a):** Therefore, our initial assumption that such a model M exists is false. No computational model based solely on the principle of quantum superposition can outperform classical computational models in discontinuum D.

**Part (b) \- Fractal Compression Potential**

1. **Computational Advantages Exist (Assumption):** Let us assume that there exists at least one computational problem Q in D for which some hypothetical computational model H can solve Q with a time complexity strictly lower than the time complexity of any algorithm running on a classical computational model.  
2. **Fractal Compression Equivalence (from S3(b)):** By Statement S3(b), a fractal compression algorithm A satisfying properties S2P1-S2P6 can simulate any observable phenomenon attributed to quantum systems within D.  
3. **Argument for Equivalence:**  
   * To outperform classical models for problem Q (as per our assumption in point 1), the hypothetical model H must leverage some computational principle or mechanism that is not available to classical models.  
   * Since fractal compression A can simulate all known quantum phenomena (point 2\) and we assume that H gains its advantage from some property present in physical reality (which fractal compression aims to model), it follows that fractal compression A could, in principle, simulate the essential computational mechanisms of H.  
   * If A can simulate the mechanisms by which H achieves its computational advantage, then A can also, in principle, achieve that same advantage.  
4. **Conclusion (b):** Therefore, if computational advantages exist in D beyond those achievable by classical models, a fractal compression algorithm satisfying S2P1-S2P6 has the potential to achieve those advantages.

[^1]:  Poincaré, H. (1890). *Sur le problème des trois corps et les équations de la dynamique*. Acta Mathematica, 13, 1–270.
